summary: Audience tape with typical 1970 hiss and pops but surprisingly listenable; the show shines with a scorching Black Peter, epic China > Rider > High Time, fierce The Other One and a monster Lovelight.
review: Even though this is a third‑generation audience recording that suffers from hiss, occasional splices and a few pitch quirks, the performance more than makes up for it. 1970’s raw energy erupts in a blistering Black Peter, an unforgettable China > Rider > High Time suite, a ferocious The Other One and a marathon Lovelight that leaves the crowd electric.  Listeners repeatedly note Jerry’s searing guitar, Phil’s thunder‑bass punches and the unstoppable drumming, making it a must‑listen for fans who can look past the tape’s technical flaws.

ai_rating.rationale: Most reviewers praise the musical performance (average ~4.5 stars) while acknowledging moderate to poor recording quality (~3 stars). Weighted average (70 % show, 30 % tape) yields a solid 4.1‑star rating.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Third‑generation AUD with noticeable hiss, pops, splices and occasional high‑pitch speed issues; crowd chatter sometimes overpowers the mix but overall intelligible and captures the concert’s intensity.

show_quality.setlist_flow: Reviewers highlight seamless transitions – Black Peter opens, the China > Rider > High Time suite ignites the second set, followed by a high‑octane NFA → Drums → Good Lovin' → The Other One → NFA → Lovelight sequence that keeps the momentum rolling.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar tone and vocal work are repeatedly praised – “mind‑numbingly fast” leads on The Other One, soaring solos on Black Peter and a beautiful voice on Good Lovin'.
band_member_comments.Phil: Phil’s bass is described as a ‘machine‑gun’ intro into Good Lovin' and a driving force that punches through the Drums segment.
band_member_comments.Bob: Weir’s rhythm/lead guitar gets kudos for “burning” work on the Rider portion of the China > Rider sequence.
band_member_comments.Keys: Pigpen’s vocal performance is highlighted as powerful (“delivers like a man”) and his stage antics add to the show’s excitement, though organ solos are largely absent.
band_member_comments.Drums: Mickey and Bill (or “the drummers”) are lauded for a tribal‑like, high‑energy Drums segment that propels the NFA → Good Lovin' transition.
